learner_goal: Map vaccine priming to a durable, faster secondary response — analogous to warming a read-through cache after a safe prefetch.
launch_attempt: A vaccine is a controlled, attenuated exposure: the immune system runs a training pass on antigen shape without paying the full pathogenic cost, so a later real infection can short-circuit to a faster effector response.
cold_attempt: Primary exposure presents antigen; B and T lineages expand; some differentiated effectors clear the signal. On re-exposure, titers rise faster and symptoms shrink — so the system must be reusing prior state, but I have not specified which cellular state is retained between encounters.
repair_dialogue_turns: If I borrow a CS frame: the first encounter writes a key (antigen epitope) and bumps counters, but I am still treating the retained artifact as circulating antibody alone — not a dedicated memory lymphocyte compartment that lowers activation latency.

Revision: clonal selection leaves a long-lived memory B/T pool. Second challenge does not cold-start from naive repertoire search; memory cells expand quickly into effectors, which is the mechanism that compresses time-to-clearance.

Concrete chain: safe prime → antigen-specific lymphocyte expansion → subset differentiates into memory cells that persist → on real re-exposure those memory cells re-activate and produce effectors faster, so the observable outcome is accelerated clearance before pathology.
gap_attempt: After the model bridge: the persisted memory lymphocyte population is the hinge — it encodes the first exposure and is what makes the second response faster, not antibody alone floating without a cellular source.
spaced_attempt: Spaced reconstruction: vaccine delivers a safe antigen preview; adaptive immune cells specific to that antigen proliferate; a fraction become long-lived memory B and T cells; when the same pathogen returns, those memory cells recognize the antigen immediately, re-enter expansion, and generate effectors with shorter lag — hence faster control and milder disease.
